epigraph.text: Unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit.
epigraph.quote: From one theorem, a universe of consequences unfolds.

content.text:
Cauchy's theorem and integral formula have extraordinary consequences. 
We present several of the most important.
Morera's theorem provides a converse to Cauchy's theorem.

content.title: Morera's Theorem
content.content:
Let $f$ be continuous on a domain $D$. If $\oint_\gamma f(z)\,dz = 0$ for 
every closed contour $\gamma$ in $D$, then $f$ is analytic on $D$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
The hypothesis implies $f$ has an antiderivative $F$ on $D$ (constructed 
by integration). Then $F' = f$, so $F$ is analytic. By the Cauchy 
Integral Formula for derivatives, $f = F'$ is also analytic.

content.title: Liouville's Theorem
content.content: If $f$ is entire and bounded, then $f$ is constant.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $|f(z)| \leq M$ for all $z$. By the Cauchy derivative formula, for 
any $z_0$ and $R > 0$:
\[
 |f'(z_0)| = \left|\frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_{|z-z_0|=R} \frac{f(z)}{(z-z_0)^2}\,dz\right| 
 \leq \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{M}{R^2} \cdot 2\pi R = \frac{M}{R}.
\]
As $R \to \infty$, $|f'(z_0)| \to 0$. Thus $f'(z_0) = 0$ for all $z_0$, 
so $f$ is constant.

content.title: Fundamental Theorem of Algebra
content.content:
Every non-constant polynomial $p(z)$ with complex coefficients has at 
least one root in $\C$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Suppose $p(z) = a_n z^n + \cdots + a_0$ has no roots. Then $f(z) = 1/p(z)$ 
is entire.

For large $|z|$, $|p(z)| \approx |a_n||z|^n \to \infty$, so 
$|f(z)| \to 0$. Thus $f$ is bounded: $|f(z)| \leq M$ for all $z$.

By Liouville's theorem, $f$ is constant. But then $p$ is constant, 
contradicting $n \geq 1$.

content.text:
This "algebraic" theorem has an essentially analytic proof—a 
beautiful example of the unity of mathematics.

content.title: Maximum Modulus Principle
content.content:
Let $f$ be analytic and non-constant on a domain $D$. Then $|f|$ has no 
local maximum in $D$. If $D$ is bounded and $f$ is continuous on 
$\overline{D}$, then $|f|$ attains its maximum on the boundary $\partial D$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
If $|f(z_0)|$ were a local maximum, the mean value property would give
\[
 |f(z_0)| = \left|\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} f(z_0 + re^{it})\,dt\right| 
 \leq \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} |f(z_0 + re^{it})|\,dt \leq |f(z_0)|.
\]
Equality requires $|f|$ to be constant on the circle, hence constant 
everywhere (by repetition), hence $f$ constant—contradiction.

content.title: Joseph Liouville (1809--1882)
content.content:
Liouville was a prolific French mathematician who worked in analysis, 
number theory, and mathematical physics. His theorem on bounded entire 
functions appears simple but has profound consequences, including the 
Fundamental Theorem of Algebra.

marginNotes.text:
"Can a man hide himself in secret 
places so that I cannot see him?" declares the Lord.

marginNotes.author: Giacinto Morera (1886)
marginNotes.text:
A function need not be assumed 
differentiable; integrals suffice.

marginNotes.author: Jacques Hadamard
marginNotes.text:
The maximum of an analytic function is 
always on the boundary.
